result:
On certain fog-heavy evenings, I walk to the decommissioned lighthouse at the end of the breakwater. The cove holds its breath, and gulls become silhouettes strung like punctuation along the railing. The lantern room is dark now, its glass panes draped in a patient film of salt. Below it, a small, tasteful cell antenna pretends not to be a crown. People stand in clusters facing the gray, taking photos of a thing that never turns anymore. My phone adjusts its brightness all by itself. I look at its single, unblinking rectangle of light and think: we have turned the beam inward.

They keep the old Fresnel lens in the maritime museum two blocks away, a cathedral of glass prisms nested like a honeycomb. The docent lays a hand on the edge of the case as if steadying a sleeping giant. She tells me about the keepers who polished each facet with flour and water, about wicks cut to a perfect taper, about the way a small flame could be caught, multiplied, and thrown miles across tide-spun night. I circle the case and see versions of myself stacked in it at eighty angles. In each one, I am both witness and echo.

All signaling is a confession: I am here, and I want to be seen. It is true of the white pulse that once marked this harbor’s shoulder. It is true of the blinking cursor in our messages, the rings and pings we make to fold distance. Out in the thicket behind my building, fireflies barter with darkness, stitching threads of coded desire. In the city’s windows, routers wink their bedside lullabies. Flowers flag ultraviolet pleas to bees. Birds whistle coordinates of belonging. A pair of kids hangs a bedsheet on a balcony and paints a blue heart. Every species invents a syntax to widen the circle.

I collect old radios from yard sales, the wooden ones with dials like ship’s wheels. At night I turn one on and listen to the surf of static. When conditions are right, signals skip from far away, glancing off layers of air like stones across a pond. Voices slither in from other coasts—sports announcers, midnight preachers, a weather robot reading barometer numbers in a perfect monotone. Once, I found a station that did nothing but count. “Five, eight, five, eight,” it whispered, a ghost of some Cold War habit. My grandfather used to put his ear to such a box and say he could feel the world turning.

The new beacons are clean and clever. My music player understands when I’m trying to stay in one mood and stretches it like taffy. The streaming sites give me the news I tolerate, rationed to keep me coming back. Maps anticipate the drive I am about to take and sigh with reassurance. So many lighthouses now, everywhere, algorithmic and tender as long as I do not stray too far. They misread me, sometimes with a weird generosity: after I search for a recipe for a friend’s dietary quirk, my ads assume I have reimagined myself. There is comfort in being predicted; there is danger too. To be correctly found is to be pinned.

On the seawall, the foghorn tests its throat—a horn of such low frequency you feel it at the hinges of your ribs. In winter storms the old journals say the keeper climbed the stairs with his boots filled with water and wrung his socks with blistered fingers. He trimmed, he wound, he watched. He wrote down the barometric dives like he was keeping score with the sky. The lens did the brilliant part, but it was his breath fogging the glass. You read those logs and understand: vigilance is an art built out of small, repetitive refusals to quit. A vow, sharpened by weather.

Alone is not always lonely, but there are days it rhymes too cleanly. In my building, the plumbing confesses our routines to each other. The neighbor’s shower hums its morning F-sharp; somewhere above, a late pan slides onto a burner. Once I found a green bottle jammed between rocks below the lighthouse, a crumbled note inside that had bled its ink into filigree. I dried it on my windowsill for days, coaxing words from stains. “We were here,” it finally told me, nothing more useful than that. I pictured the sender without picturing their face. It was enough. The bottle, like all signals, had been a wish disguised as evidence.

A quarter mile off the beach, in summer, kids tie glow sticks to their wrists and dive, each arc a comet in the swell. On certain nights, bioluminescent plankton bloom and everything touched becomes briefly mythic. You kick and the water writes a sentence around your legs. You lift your hand and shake blue sparks from your fingertips. I have put my head under then and blown bubbles like punctuating syllables just to watch the grammar of light exhale. The sea replies with a diluted cloud, then is dark again. It is easy to believe, for a minute, that communication can be this pure, that every stroke leaves a legible trace.

Streetlights have become so uniformly white that the city feels as if it has been erased and redrawn with a crisp pen. The moths are confused. The astronomers complain that the color of the sky has changed, and the migrating birds stitch new margins into their maps. At the museum, the retired keeper sits on a folding chair by the lens and folds his hands like a prayer that got tired. He tells me about nights when fog swallowed sound and you could not hear your own footsteps. He says he counted. He says counting was sometimes a way of keeping fear on a leash. When he laughs, it is with the part of the voice that survived weather.

Later, I stand on my balcony with a mug of tea and watch the tiny sentinels blinking around the building: routers behind curtains, ovens thinking, cars dreaming softly to themselves in their parking spaces. Someone across the alley opens a fridge and the white light steps forward like a minor revelation. In my own kitchen, a cord of LEDs breathes along the counter. The moon is a slim bruise of light, our oldest borrowed signal, a borrowed voice still patient with us. A breeze leans its face into the wind chimes and makes a little punctuation. In this fairground of domestic constellations, I remember the message that all beacons mumble in their different dialects: not Stop, or Go, or Arrive Soon, but simply, persistently, You are not alone.

Once, after I left the museum, I saw the keeper on the bus staring at his hands as if they were maps. He did not know me. I wanted to ask if he ever missed the night at full cry, the urgent beam, the way a foghorn could carry your thoughts farther than you meant to allow. But he got off before the question arranged itself. In the empty seat, a circle of warmth remained, a private beacon dissolving quickly. The bus made its way past the dark tower and turned up the hill, and every window along the route flickered its specific blue, an archipelago of little promises. I traced them with my finger on the fogged glass, a kind of quiet alchemy: a street translated into a sky, every room a lighthouse, every lighthouse a room. I let the pattern hold for three stops, then wiped the window clear and watched the night revert to itself, the sea breathing in a patient meter, the city whispering back in lights.

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 1000 words.
